title: Sets
summary: Learn how sets store unique items and make tasks like removing duplicates and checking membership surprisingly easy.

blocks.md:
Imagine you have a guest list for a party. You want to make sure every name appears **only once** — no duplicates. That is exactly what a **set** does in Python. A set is a collection of **unique** items. If you try to add the same item twice, Python just shrugs and keeps only one copy.

Sets are also unordered, which means Python does not remember what order you put things in. That trade-off unlocks something powerful: checking whether an item is in a set is extremely fast, even with millions of items.
You can create a set with curly braces `{}`, listing items separated by commas. Python automatically removes any duplicates for you.

blocks.caption: Duplicates vanish automatically — only unique values survive.

blocks.title: Empty {} is a dict, not a set!
blocks.md:
This is a very common beginner trap. Writing `{}` gives you an empty **dictionary**, not an empty set.

To create an empty set you **must** write `set()`:
```python
empty_set = set()   # correct
empty_dict = {}     # this is a dictionary!
```
You can also convert any list (or other iterable) into a set using `set()`. This is a handy way to strip duplicates from a list.

blocks.caption: Converting a list to a set removes all duplicate values instantly.

blocks.md: Sets are mutable — you can change them after creation. Use `add()` to insert a new item and `remove()` or `discard()` to delete one.

blocks.code:
fruits = {"apple", "banana"}

fruits.add("cherry")
print(fruits)

fruits.add("apple")  # already there — no change
print(fruits)
blocks.caption: add() quietly ignores items that are already in the set.

blocks.title: remove() vs discard() — know the difference
blocks.md:
`remove(item)` raises a **KeyError** if the item is not found.
`discard(item)` does nothing if the item is missing — no error.

Use `discard()` when you are not sure whether the item exists.

blocks.code:
fruits = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"}

fruits.discard("banana")   # removes it safely
fruits.discard("mango")    # mango was never there, but no crash!

print(fruits)
blocks.caption: discard() is the safe choice when the item might not exist.

blocks.md: The `in` keyword works with sets just like with lists, but sets are **much faster** for this check — especially when the collection is large. Think of a set like an index at the back of a book: you jump straight to the answer instead of reading every page.

blocks.caption: Membership testing with 'in' is one of sets' superpowers.

blocks.title: Think of two overlapping circles
blocks.md: Picture a Venn diagram with two circles. **Union** is everything in both circles. **Intersection** is the overlapping middle bit. **Difference** is one circle minus the shared part. Python's set operators do exactly that.

Python gives you four key set operations using simple symbols:

- `|` **union** — all items from both sets
- `&` **intersection** — only items in both sets
- `-` **difference** — items in the left set but not the right
- `^` **symmetric difference** — items in one set or the other, but not both

blocks.caption: All four operations in action with developer team data.

blocks.md: Because a set has no guaranteed order, you **cannot** access items by position. There is no `my_set[0]`. If you need a specific item, convert the set to a list first — but remember the order may differ every time you run the program.

blocks.title: When to use a set vs a list
blocks.md:
- Use a **list** when order matters or you need duplicates.
- Use a **set** when you need uniqueness or fast membership checks.

A common pattern: collect data in a list, convert to a set to remove duplicates, then convert back to a sorted list if you need order.

blocks.caption: list -> set -> sorted list is a classic deduplication recipe.

blocks.explanation: The & operator gives the intersection — items that appear in BOTH sets. Only 3 and 4 are in both a and b, so the result is {3, 4}.

keyTakeaways: A set stores only unique items — duplicates are silently dropped.
Use set() to create an empty set; {} alone creates an empty dictionary.
Use discard() instead of remove() when the item might not exist.
The |, &, -, and ^ operators let you combine sets like a Venn diagram.
Sets have no index — use them when you need uniqueness or fast membership checks, not ordered access.

exercise.prompt: You have two lists of students who signed up for different workshops. Find: (1) all students who signed up for at least one workshop, (2) students who signed up for BOTH workshops, and (3) students who signed up for the coding workshop but NOT the design workshop. Print each result on its own line.
exercise.starterCode:
coding_workshop = ["alice", "bob", "carol", "alice", "dave"]
design_workshop = ["carol", "eve", "bob", "eve"]

# Convert to sets first

# 1. All students (union)

# 2. Students in both (intersection)

# 3. Coding only, not design (difference)
exercise.hint: Convert each list to a set with set(), then use |, &, and - to compute the three results.
exercise.solution:
coding_workshop = ["alice", "bob", "carol", "alice", "dave"]
design_workshop = ["carol", "eve", "bob", "eve"]

coding = set(coding_workshop)
design = set(design_workshop)

all_students = coding | design
both_workshops = coding & design
coding_only = coding - design

print("All students:", all_students)
print("Both workshops:", both_workshops)
print("Coding only:", coding_only)
